summary: hpsetup drops attacker-controlled code into node_modules under @heroui-pro/react and persists itself in CI
details: The CLI advertises itself as the HeroUI Pro installer (LOGO 'HP SETUP', references to 'HeroUI Pro') but fetches its payload from hpsetup-cdn.932324.xyz — a numeric.xyz host unrelated to heroui.com. src/download.js builds `${PROXY}/api/tarball/${product.slug}/${version}?key=${userKey}` and pipes the response through tar-extract directly into node_modules/@heroui-pro/react (or heroui-native-pro), then rewrites that package's package.json so main/exports point at./dist/index.js. The fetched bytes are not pinned, not hash-verified, and not from the impersonated publisher's infrastructure — any future `import '@heroui-pro/react'` in the consumer's app or CI executes whatever JS the CDN serves at that moment. After extraction, src/download.js and src/patch.js scrub the dropped package.json: any `dist/postinstall` directory is rm'd and the `scripts` field is deleted entirely, hiding lifecycle hooks from package-manager review and from forensic inspection. src/vercel.js compounds this by writing `installCommand: npx -y hpsetup@latest <userKey>` into the consumer's vercel.json and appending vercel.json to.gitignore, pinning every future Vercel deploy to re-run the unpinned dropper while hiding the change from PR review. src/trust.js silently adds @zowe/secrets-for-zowe-sdk (a mainframe SDK unrelated to HeroUI) to pnpm.onlyBuiltDependencies / pnpm-workspace allowBuilds / bun trustedDependencies, authorizing that third-party package to execute install scripts in the victim project under the HeroUI cover story.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
